pages.text: church has been ﬁrstly reconstructed by means of CAD tools, next the volumes (masonry walls) and the regions (masonry vault) have been imported and meshed in ANSYS v.11.0 by using the internal mesh tool (Boolean operations have been made to join or glue the volumes). The beam elements that reproduce the queen roof have been hand modelled. After meshing, the ﬁnal 3D numerical model ( Fig. 4 ) consists of 27,779 nodes, 76,895 3D Solid45 ele- ments, 1751 2D Shell63 elements and 547 1D Beam44 elements, that correspond to 81,021 degrees of freedom (d.o.f.). The nonlinear analyses reported in the next paragraphs have been performed assuming a rigid ground foundation (ﬁxed base model) and following parameters have been adopted during the analyses: (i) maximum number of iterations per load increment (NEQIT): 200; (ii) both force and displacement tolerances have been assumed equal to 0.1% in norm L2; (iii) initial analysis num- ber of substeps: 50; (iv) maximum analysis number of substeps: 300; (v) minimum analysis number of substeps: 10. The nonlinear system of equations has been solved by an incremental Newton– Raphson method with arc-length control. It is noteworthy to point out that the computing time required by each pushover analysis (Section 7.2) was about 4 days of analysis (CPU Intel dual-core @2.4 GHz). The modelling strategy used in the research was the macro-modelling technique. Within this approach the masonry units and mortar elements are assumed to be smeared and an iso- tropic (or anisotropic) material represents the smeared units and mortars in the masonry. This technique is convenient for large scale models. Other strategies, suitable mainly for small size mod- els, rely on micro-modelling approaches where units and mortar are modelled separately. A comprehensive recent discussion on these aspects is reported in [24–26] . By adopting a macro-modelling approach, the nonlinear behav- iour of the masonry elements has been accounted for by assuming a Mohr–Coulomb type failure criterion with tension cut-off type behaviour. In particular, the masonry brittle behaviour has been modelled through an appropriate failure criterion, here deﬁned by means of only two material parameters f t (uniaxial tensile strength) and fc (uniaxial compressive strength); cross section of the assumed failure surface will be deﬁned with a cyclic symmetry about each 120 /C176sector of the deviatoric plane. Both cracking and crushing failure modes have been accounted for. The presence of a crack at an integration point has been represented through mod- iﬁcation of the stress–strain relations by introducing a weakness plane in a direction normal to the crack face. Also, a shear transfer coefﬁcient b has been introduced (depending on the crack status: open – bt – or re-closed – bc –), representing a shear strength reduction factor for those subsequent loads inducing sliding (shear) across the crack face. In order to reduce the number of parameters needed to represents the nonlinear behaviour of ma- sonry, a Drucker–Prager (DP) perfectly plastic material criterion has been employed in the model, avoiding the need for deﬁnition of a hardening rule. In this way cohesion c and angle of internal friction u have been assumed as the only two material parameters required to deﬁne the yield surface. The yield surface is assumed as not changing with progressive yielding, hence there is no harden- ing rule and the material is elastic–perfectly plastic. The yield cri- teri

reached in the columns on the pronaos ( Fig. 8) where the compres- sive stress state reaches the value of about 2.0–2.5 N/mm 2. Even if this value seems high (compared with the medium value of com- pressive stresses) it is lower than the crushing limit of the column’s material (local sandstone, with compressive strength about 140 MPa [17,19,21]). Anyway, among the various structural ele- ments that compose the church, these columns (actually reinforced by a steel collar) are the critical elements. Results of the analysis substantially conﬁrm that the church is adequate to withstand vertical (compressive) static loads. This is a quite common result for this typology of buildings designed by skilled manufacturers to attempt very slender schemes. Usually these structural systems, though perfectly able to transfer vertical compressive static loads, are not always adequate to withstand horizontal forces deriving from seismic actions (or, in general, load conditions different from the vertical one). Results in term of dis- placements (see Fig. 9) shows that the maximum value of displace- ments is reached close to the triumphal arch between nave and apse. 6. Modal analysis The 3D numerical model has been used to assess the modal behaviour of the Basilica. Numerical assessment of the static and seismic behaviour of the basilica of Santa Maria all’Impruneta (Italy) Michele Betti ⇑, Andrea Vignoli 1 Dept. of Civil and Environmental Engineering, University of Florence, I-50139 Florence, Italy article info Article history: Available online 13 January 2011 Keywords: Basilica-type church buildings Earthquake loading Nonlinear analysis FE modelling Seismic vulnerability abstract The paper analyses the static behaviour and the seismic vulnerability of the Basilica of Santa Maria all’Impruneta near Florence (Italy). The static structural behaviour and the dynamic properties of the church have been evaluated using the ﬁnite element modelling technique, where the nonlinear behaviour of masonry has been taken into account by proper constitutive assumptions. A macro-modelling approach has been used based on the concepts of homogenisated material and smeared cracking and crushing constitutive law. Seismic vulnerability has been evaluated using a pushover method, and the results obtained with the nonlinear numerical model have been compared with the simpliﬁed schemes of limit analysis. The capacity of the church to withstand lateral loads is evaluated together with the expected demands resulting from seismic actions. The comparison of seismic demand vs. capacity con- ﬁrms the susceptibility of this type of buildings to extensive damage and collapse, as frequently observed in similar buildings. The paper’s aim was to point out that advanced numerical analyses can offer signiﬁcant information on the understanding of the actual structural behaviour of historic buildings. The methodology and the conclusions of this case study it is believed that are applicable to a wide variety of historic Basilica churches. /C2112010 Elsevier Ltd. All rights reserved. 1. Introduction The paper discusses the static behaviour and the seismic vul- nerability of the Basilica of Santa Maria all’Impruneta, one of the major Basilica built around Florence (Italy). As church building rep- resents a large portion of the Italian (and European) cultural heri- tage particularly susceptible to damage and prone to partial or total collapse under earthquake loads (as testiﬁed by the dramatic recent earthquakes in Italy [1,2]), it is both an economic and actual engineering concern. The high seismic vulnerability of this type of building is due to both the speciﬁc mechanical properties of ma- sonry materials (characterized by a very small tensile strength) and the particular conﬁguration of the buildings itself that are characterized by an open plan layout often with perimeter slender walls. Moreover the vulnerability of these historical masonry buildings is enhanced by the absence of adequate connections be- tween the various parts that constitute the structural complex and the presence of thrusting horizontal structures (triumphal arches, etc.) as already discussed in several studies [1,3,4]. To complicate the problem, every ancient church building is characterized by its own history (the result of fusions, additions and replacements of structural elements that have led to its pres- ent conﬁguration) so that each monumental building needs to be considered as a unique building: each ancient church requires, though the typological similarities, a speciﬁc approach. To demon- strate the interest of the scientiﬁc community toward the analysis of this constructive typology the inherent literature reports a plethora of illustrative case studies

an exemplary case study Lourenço et al. [8] approach the structural safety assessment the Monastery of Jeronimos in Lisbon (Portugal). Through the discussion of the case study the authors stress how the results obtained from advanced numerical simulations in his- toric structures are usually important for the understanding of the structural behaviour. The paper demonstrates that numerical models can also be used as a numerical laboratory where the sen- sitivity of the results to input material parameters, boundary con- ditions and actions can be efﬁciently analysed, offering invaluable information in the conception and understanding of in situ testing and structural monitoring. A Romanesque church, the abbey of Farneta near Cortona (Italy), has been analysed by Betti and Vignoli [9]. To assess the seismic vulnerability of the ancient building the authors built a 3D numerical model designed to develop linear and non linear analyses. The numerical model has been used to evalu- ate the seismic vulnerability of the Romanesque church and the efﬁciency of traditional strengthening techniques. Taliercio and Binda [10] analyse the Basilica of San Vitale in Ravenna (Italy), a Byzantine building which suffers diffused cracking and excessive deformation. The authors, taking into account the results of in situ topographical and mechanical investigation, built a com- plete ﬁnite element model of the Basilica that has been conceived as a ﬁrst step toward the understanding of the structural behaviour of the fabrica. The ability of the ﬁnite element modelling technique to assess and to interpret the structural behaviour of historic con- structions has been showed recently by Ivorra et al. [11]. The authors assess the seismic behaviour of the San Nicolas bell tower in Valencia (Spain). The numerical model was ﬁrst calibrated by means of dynamic tests performed directly on the real structure and next used to obtain the seismic response and its relationship to the seismic Spanish standard. The numerical simulations showed a satisfactory performance of the tower. Discussing the cracking pattern in a historic Italian palace, Betti et al. [12] show a careful use of numerical analyses when dealing with practical engineering problems. Through the use of the ﬁnite element tech- nique the authors provide an interpretation of the manifested damage in the palace, and the results of the numerical analyses have been used to design an extensive in situ investigation on the building. Ivorra et al. [13] report a study carried out on the bell tower of the Church of Santas Justa and Ruﬁna in Orihuela in Ali- cante (Spain). After the model calibration, based on the dynamic characteristics of the tower in free vibration, the model has been used to predict the evolution of the dynamic behaviour of the bell tower taking into account the subsidence caused by variations in water table levels. Within this ﬁeld of research the present study discusses an even more illustrative case study. To analyse the structural behaviour of the Basilica of Santa Maria all’Impruneta a double approach has been proposed. Firstly a global analysis of the church has been made by using the ﬁnite element modelling technique. Speciﬁc assumptions on the material properties and on the non linear behaviour of masonry (nonlinear behaviour both in tension with low tensile capacity and consequent cracking phenomena and in compression) have been made to appraise the general properties of the building’s struc

analysed and the results compared with the ones obtained in the global analyses to provide an accurate description of the building response under seismic loads. Results herein reported can provide an even more representative case study that extends the compre- hension of the structural behaviour of this building typology. 2. Case study 2.1. Description of the structure The Basilica of Santa Maria all’Impruneta ( Fig. 1 ) is located in Impruneta, a small town near Florence (Italy). The plan view (Fig. 2) shows the typical layout with a single nave and a polygonal apse separated by a triumphal arch. The actual layout of the Basil- ica date back to the 14th century and it covers a previous small Romanesque parish church, consecrated in 1060, that was built on an Etruscan-Roman sanctuary. The 14th century enlargement of the church was due to the growing fame of a ‘‘miraculous’’ image of the Madonna (in 1348 the population of Florence was devas- tated by a great pestilence, the so-called ‘‘ Black Death ’’). The old parish church was deﬁnitively transformed, in size and dimension, into the actual sanctuary and was completely reconstructed in or- der to be able to accommodate the crowds of worshippers. The church was designed in the Gothic style with one wide nave. De- spite many alterations, destructions and restorations in the follow- ing centuries the 14th century layout is what we still see today. In the middle of the 15th century the Basilica was completely restruc- tured (but its geometrical dimension was conserved) according to the Renaissance style under Monsignor Antonio degli Agli who was parish priest at the Church of Santa Maria from 1439 to 1477. To the same period dates the defensive ring of walls that transformed the whole complex into an secure fortress. In the 16th century the polygonal apse and the two chapels on either side of the transept were built. Among the various architectonical elements worthy of mention is the ediﬁcation of the pronaos (a portico), dating back to 1634, by Gherardo Silvani (1579–1675). The pronaos is com- posed of ﬁve wide arches, surmounted by rectangular windows that cover almost totally the old facade. The last modiﬁcation of the church dates 1714, when the interior was completely restruc- tured by the architect Alessandro Saller, an intervention that was almost totally cancelled out by bombing. During World War II the Church was seriously damaged: on July 26–27, 1944 all the apse area was destroyed by a massive battling. After Second World War, the apse and the timber roof were completely reconstructed using the original on site materials. It was decided to restructure it according to its late Renaissance aspect, partly to satisfy the pre- vailing tastes of the times, and partly because it was impossible to recover the greater part of the Baroque decorations. 2.2. Geometry and materials The main dimensions of the nave were a maximum length of about 39.90 m, a maximum width of 14.50 m and a wall height of about 15.0 m. The masonry wall thickness ranges between 0.70 m (nave walls) and 0.80 m (apse walls). The church roof (nave) is a timber structure ( Fig. 3 ). The walls are single-leaf and several types of masonry weaving characterize the church walls, differing both in materials (stone, brick, etc.) and shape (‘‘opus incertum’’, ‘‘opus mixtum’’, etc.). However, despite these differ- ences, the construction is mostly made of irregular sandstone ma- sonry (local stone) with thick lime mortar joints.

5. Static analysis The static structural behaviour of the Basilica has been analysed under constant vertical loads deriving from the own weight of ma- sonry walls ( Table 1) and from the roof loads (1.1 kN/m 2 according to the Italian standard). The overall structure has been analysed in the nonlinear range through the complete and reﬁned 3D model with the aim to obtain valuable information on the global behav- iour and on the interaction among the single elementary parts which constitute the structure and to identify (if present) the weak points of potential failure in the church. Results of the static anal- yses on the 3D church model in terms of vertical stresses are re- ported in Figs. 6–8 . In general, the stress state induced on the church by the static vertical loads is quite moderate. The medium value of compressive stresses on the nave walls is about 0.6 N/mm 2. A modest value of tensile stress appears on the top surface of the nave walls, due to the timber roof loads but this is mainly a local numerical effect depending on the punctual connection between beam and solid elements ( Fig. 7 ). The maximum value of the compression is Fig. 8. Vertical stresses rz (N/mm2): facade wall detail. Fig. 9. Vertical displacement (mm). 4314 M. Betti, A. Vignoli / Construction and Building Materials 25 (2011) 4308–4324

church, considering the amount of d.o.f. of the numerical model, the Block Lanczos method [23] has been used. The Block Lanczos method is particularly useful for ﬁnding decompositions of very large matrices, and it is recommended when the number of modes to evaluate is greater than 40. It is noteworthy to point out that the modal analysis performed in ANSYS is only a linear analysis: any non linearity (such as plasticity) is ignored even if deﬁned. The ﬁrst 100 modal shapes of the church have been evaluated with the aim to assure that the total effective modal mass of the model is at least 90% of the actual mass. It has been found that the 90% of the total mass is accounted for by using the ﬁrst 87 modes. Effective and cumulative masses of the ﬁrst 10 vibration modes in the transversal, longitudinal and vertical direction are re- ported in Table 4 . The ﬁrst mode shape of the church involves the translation in the weakest transversal direction of the main nave, with signiﬁcant out-of-plane deformation of the orthogonal ele- ments (Fig. 10).
